title: Use T-SQL to convert following JSON data to a table

turns.speaker: user
assistant
turns.text:
Assuming the JSON data is stored in a variable called @json, the following T-SQL code can be used to convert it to a table:

-- Parse JSON data and store in a table variable
DECLARE @data TABLE (
    orgId VARCHAR(50),
    timestamp DATETIME,
    insuredCode VARCHAR(50),
    agentCode VARCHAR(50),
    productCode VARCHAR(50),
    branchCode VARCHAR(50),
    applicantType VARCHAR(50),
    applicantNameEn VARCHAR(50),
    sameAsApplicant VARCHAR(50),
    insuredAge INT,
    riskCode VARCHAR(50),
    riskType VARCHAR(50)
)
INSERT INTO @data (orgId, timestamp, insuredCode, agentCode, productCode, branchCode, applicantType, applicantNameEn, sameAsApplicant, insuredAge, riskCode, riskType)
SELECT
    JSON_VALUE(@json, '$.orgId') AS orgId,
    CONVERT(DATETIME, JSON_VALUE(@json, '$.timestamp'), 120) AS timestamp,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.insuredCode') AS insuredCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.agentCode') AS agentCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.productCode') AS productCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.branchCode') AS branchCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.applicant.applicantType') AS applicantType,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.applicant.applicantNameEn') AS applicantNameEn,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.insuredList[0].sameAsApplicant') AS sameAsApplicant,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.insuredList[0].insuredAge') AS insuredAge,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.riskList[0].riskCode') AS riskCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.riskList[0].riskType') AS riskType
FROM OPENJSON(@json, '$.overdueList') list

-- View the table
SELECT * FROM @data

This will convert the JSON data to a table with columns orgId, timestamp, insuredCode, agentCode, productCode, branchCode, applicantType, applicantNameEn, sameAsApplicant, insuredAge, riskCode, and riskType. Note that the sameAsApplicant and insuredAge fields are extracted from arrays within the JSON data using square bracket notation.

turns.speaker: assistant
user
turns.text: insuredList is a list. Should not hardcode $.riskList[0]

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
You are correct, in situations where there may be multiple items within the "insuredList" or "riskList" arrays, hardcoding "$.insuredList[0]" or "$.riskList[0]" may not be appropriate. Here's the updated T-SQL code that can handle the situation when there are multiple items within the arrays:

-- Parse JSON data and store in a table variable
DECLARE @data TABLE (
    orgId VARCHAR(50),
    timestamp DATETIME,
    insuredCode VARCHAR(50),
    agentCode VARCHAR(50),
    productCode VARCHAR(50),
    branchCode VARCHAR(50),
    applicantType VARCHAR(50),
    applicantNameEn VARCHAR(50),
    sameAsApplicant VARCHAR(50),
    insuredAge INT,
    riskCode VARCHAR(50),
    riskType VARCHAR(50)
)
INSERT INTO @data (orgId, timestamp, insuredCode, agentCode, productCode, branchCode, applicantType, applicantNameEn, sameAsApplicant, insuredAge, riskCode, riskType)
SELECT
    JSON_VALUE(@json, '$.orgId') AS orgId,
    CONVERT(DATETIME, JSON_VALUE(@json, '$.timestamp'), 120) AS timestamp,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.insuredCode') AS insuredCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.agentCode') AS agentCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.productCode') AS productCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.branchCode') AS branchCode,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.applicant.applicantType') AS applicantType,
    JSON_VALUE(list.value, '$.applicant.applicantNameEn') AS applicantNameEn,
    insuredList.sameAsApplicant,
    insuredList.insuredAge,
    riskList.riskCode,
    riskList.riskType
FROM OPENJSON(@json, '$.overdueList') list
CROSS APPLY OPENJSON(list.value, '$.insuredList') WITH (
    sameAsApplicant VARCHAR(50) '$.sameAsApplicant',
    insuredAge INT '$.insuredAge'
) insuredList
CROSS APPLY OPENJSON(list.value, '$.riskList') WITH (
    riskCode VARCHAR(50) '$.riskCode',
    riskType VARCHAR(50) '$.riskType'
) riskList

-- View the table
SELECT * FROM @data

In this updated code, CROSS APPLY function is used in combination with the OPENJSON function to extract the "sameAsApplicant" and "insuredAge" fields from each "insuredList" item, and the "riskCode" and "riskType" fields from each "riskList" item, respectively. The function applies the extracted fields to each row of the table, resulting in a properly formatted table that reflects the structure of the original JSON data.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: what is the best method to create ETL program for free

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
There are several free and open-source ETL tools available that you can use to create ETL programs. Here are some of the most popular ones:

1. Apache NiFi: An easy-to-use web-based tool for data ingestion, transformation, and routing. It supports a wide range of data sources and destinations, and has a drag-and-drop interface for building data workflows. NiFi also has powerful features for data security, auditing, and monitoring.

2. Talend Open Studio: A powerful and flexible ETL tool that supports a wide range of data integration needs. Talend Open Studio has a drag-and-drop interface for designing data workflows, and supports a wide range of data sources and destinations, including Big Data platforms such as Hadoop and Spark.

3. Pentaho Data Integration: Another popular open-source ETL tool with a drag-and-drop interface for designing data workflows. Pentaho Data Integration supports a wide range of data sources and destinations, and has powerful features for data profiling, cleansing, and transformation.

4. StreamSets Data Collector: An easy-to-use open-source tool for building data pipelines. StreamSets Data Collector has a drag-and-drop interface for designing data workflows, and supports a wide range of data sources and destinations. It also has built-in data quality and validation features.

5. PySpark: Although not a GUI-based tool, PySpark is a powerful ETL engine that can be used to build data pipelines using Python code. PySpark is part of the Apache Spark project, and supports parallel processing of large datasets.

All of these tools are free to use and have active communities supporting them. The best one for you will depend on your specific needs and the complexity of your data workflows.
